coredata.dc:title: A reliable and selective ratiometric sensing probe for fluorometric determination of P2O7 4− based on AIE of GSH@CuNCs-assisted by Al-N@CQDs
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mahmoud, Ashraf M.
Abu-Alrub, Samer S.
Al-Qarni, Ali O.
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Shahin, Reem Y.
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel composite material was developed for the ratiometric detection of pyrophosphate anion (P2O7 4−). This composite consisted of Al and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Al-N@CQDs) and glutathione-capped copper nanoclusters (GSH@CuNCs). The Al-N@CQDs component, with its high reserved coordination capacity of Al3+, induced the non-luminescent behavior of GSH@CuNCs, resulting in an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect. The hybrid material (Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs) exhibited dual-emission signals at 620 nm and 450 nm after integrating the two independent materials utilizing the AIE effect and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) approach. This approach represents the first utilization of this composite for ratiometric detection. Nevertheless, upon the addition of P2O7 4−, the AIE and FRET processes were hindered due to the higher coordination interaction of Al3+ towards P2O7 4− compared to the amino/carboxyl groups on Al-N@CQDs. This successful interference of the AIE and FRET processes allowed for the effective estimation of P2O7 4−. The response ratio (F450/F620) increased with increasing the concentration of P2O7 4− in the range of 0.035–160 µM, with an impressive detection limit of 0.012 µM. This innovative approach of utilizing hybrid CQDs/thiolate-capped nanoclusters as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for analytical applications introduces new possibilities in the field. The as-fabricated system was successfully applied to detect P2O7 4− in different real samples such as water, serum, and urine samples with acceptable results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyrophosphate
Al-N@CQDs
CuNCs
Aggregation-induced emission
Coordination interaction

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code ( NU/NRP/MRC/12/19 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of Al-N@CQDs (A) and GSH@CuNCs (C). (B) and (D) are size distribution of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs, respectively.
(A) FTIR spectra of N@CQDs (a) and Al-N@CQDs (b); (B) FTIR spectra of GSH@CuNCs (a) and CuNCs (b).
XRD patterns of Al-N@CQDs (A) and GSH@CuNCs (B).
(A) UV/Vis absorption spectra of N@CQDs, Al-@ N@CQDs, and GSH@CuNCs; (B) Fluorescence spectra of N@CQDs, Al-@ N@CQDs, and GSH@CuNCs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system after addition of different concentrations of P2O7 4− (0.035–160 µM); (B) Calibration plot between F450/F620 versus concentration of P2O7 4− under optimum conditions.

Selectivity of the Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs towards the detection of 10 µM P2O7 4− in the presence of (A) 1 mM Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, 50 mM Ba2+, and 20 μM anions; (B) 100 µM biomolecules under optimized conditions.
Main steps for preparation of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs hybrid and detection of P2O7 4− using the proposed strategy.
Analytical performance of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs and other reported fluorometric sensors for the detection of P2O7 4− anion.
Applications of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system for detection of P2O7 4− anion in water, serum, and urine samples (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A reliable and selective ratiometric sensing probe for fluorometric determination of P2O7 4− based on AIE of GSH@CuNCs-assisted by Al-N@CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, College of Pharmacy, Najran University, Najran, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Sphinx University, New Assiut City, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Sphinx University, New Assiut City, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel composite material was developed for the ratiometric detection of pyrophosphate anion (P2O7 4−). This composite consisted of Al and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (Al-N@CQDs) and glutathione-capped copper nanoclusters (GSH@CuNCs). The Al-N@CQDs component, with its high reserved coordination capacity of Al3+, induced the non-luminescent behavior of GSH@CuNCs, resulting in an aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect. The hybrid material (Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs) exhibited dual-emission signals at 620 nm and 450 nm after integrating the two independent materials utilizing the AIE effect and the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) approach. This approach represents the first utilization of this composite for ratiometric detection. Nevertheless, upon the addition of P2O7 4−, the AIE and FRET processes were hindered due to the higher coordination interaction of Al3+ towards P2O7 4− compared to the amino/carboxyl groups on Al-N@CQDs. This successful interference of the AIE and FRET processes allowed for the effective estimation of P2O7 4−. The response ratio (F450/F620) increased with increasing the concentration of P2O7 4− in the range of 0.035–160 µM, with an impressive detection limit of 0.012 µM. This innovative approach of utilizing hybrid CQDs/thiolate-capped nanoclusters as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for analytical applications introduces new possibilities in the field. The as-fabricated system was successfully applied to detect P2O7 4− in different real samples such as water, serum, and urine samples with acceptable results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The pyrophosphate anion (P2O7 4−) has garnered significant attention due to its involvement in various biochemical activities, including energy transduction in cells, DNA replication, and metabolic processes [1–3]. Therefore, the accurate measurement of P2O7 4− levels in organisms holds great importance for disease diagnosis [4,5]. Additionally, water eutrophication caused by certain food additives and the electroplating industry poses a threat to both the economy and human health, increasing the disease burden [6]. Studies have reported a significant accumulation of P2O7 4− in the synovial fluids of individuals with pseudogout (calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate deposition) [7]. Conversely, its deficiency has been strongly linked to Monckeberg's arteriosclerosis [8]. Moreover, the level of P2O7 4− has been shown to be closely associated with cancer diagnosis, emphasizing its crucial role in vital processes like DNA replication [9]. Based on these facts, it is crucial to develop selective, reliable, and sensitive analytical methods for the determination of P2O7 4− in various sample matrices. Many analytical techniques were developed for the detection of P2O7 4− such as dual-energy computed tomography [10], SERS [11], voltammetry [12], and dark field optical microscope [13]. Most of these approaches have limited applicability due to lengthy pretreatment stages, a lack of selectivity (e.g., voltammetry), and the need of specialized instrumentation. As a result, analytical procedures that minimize the high cost and the complexity of data collecting are required. In contrast, fluorometric methods are featured by rapid detection response, sensitivity, sufficient selectivity, simplicity, and low-cost [14,15].

Researchers are increasingly interested in carbon quantum dots (CQDs), a type of carbon nanomaterials characterized by their small size (<10 nm) [16]. Their remarkable properties, such as ease of manufacturing, surface functionalization, small particle size, biocompatibility, low toxicity, high quantum yield, and excellent photo-stability, have contributed to their growing popularity [17,18]. The presence of heteroatom doping on their surface results in good water solubility, high photoluminescence quantum yield, and ease-of-surface modifications [19,20]. Ratiometric-based determinations find applications in various scientific fields, including biochemistry, molecular biology, environmental monitoring, and clinical diagnostics. It enables quantitative analysis and detection of substances of interest, even in complex samples, by utilizing the relative changes in signals rather than relying solely on absolute values [21–23].

Metal nanoclusters (MNCs) often display interesting optical and electronic properties [24–26]. They can exhibit size-dependent luminescence, with emission wavelengths ranging from the ultraviolet to the near-infrared region [27]. Their high surface area and unique electronic structure enable enhanced reactivity and selectivity, making them valuable in areas like energy conversion, organic synthesis, and environmental remediation [28]. They can be functionalized with specific ligands or biomolecules, enabling their application in biomedicine. They can be used for drug delivery systems, bioimaging agents, and theranostics. Their small size allows them to penetrate cellular barriers and target specific tissues or cells, offering potential advancements in cancer treatment and bio-imaging technologies [29]. Protection of MNCs with molecules such as glutathione (GSH) improves their stability and makes them ideal candidates for measuring metal ions fluorometrically via a process called “aggregation-induced emission enhancement” [30,31].

This research work introduces a reliable method for fluorometric sensing of P2O7 4− using a ratiometric approach. The sensor utilized in this study consists of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs. The interaction between Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs is regulated by AIE and FRET. These processes are hindered upon the addition of the P2O7 4− anion, leading to a decrease in the fluorescence emission from Al-N@CQDs and restoration of fluorescence from GSH@CuNCs. Importantly, this research represents the first-ever demonstration of ratiometric determination of the P2O7 4− anion utilizing the hybrid system of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs.

Tetrasodium pyrophosphate (97.7 %), glutathione (98.5 %), ciprofloxacin (98.9 %), uric acid (98.7 %), dopamine (98.7 %), cysteine (97.8 %), glucose (98.8 %), and Tris HCl buffer were procured from Sigma Aldrich. Aluminum chloride hexahydrate (AlCl3·6H2O), copper sulfate pentahydrate (CuSO4·5H2O), HCl, NaOH, and ethanol were procured from Merck.

Fluorescent intensity measurements were conducted using Shimadzu RF-5301 PC (Tokyo, Japan). UV–Vis absorbance readings were performed using the Shimadzu 1601 PC (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained using the Thermo EscalAB 250Xi instrument (Thermoelectric Corporation, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired using the NicoletTM iSTM10 spectrograph (Varian Instrument Co., Ltd, USA). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) and zeta potential measurements were recorded using the ZEN 3600 Nano ZS model (Malvern, UK). Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, JEM-100CX II-unit, USA) was used to characterize Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs. The X-ray diffraction spectrometer PW 1710 (XRD, Philips-FEI, Netherlands) was utilized to investigate the peak diffractions of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs.

Ciprofloxacin (40 mg) and AlCl3·6H2O (70 mg) were sonicated in 40 mL ultrapure water for 10 min. The resultant solution was heated at 200 °C for 10 h in a Teflon autoclave. After cooling, the product was purified using 0.22 μm membrane followed by dialysis (MWCO: 2KDa) for 24 h. The final product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C.

The synthesis of GSH@CuNCs was conducted following the procedure outlined in the literature [32]. In summary, a solution of 10 mL containing 60.0 mM GSH was carefully combined with 10 mL of 15.0 mM CuSO4·5H2O. The pH of the mixture was then adjusted to 4 by gradually adding 1.5 M NaOH until the solution turned a light yellow color. Subsequently, the solution was incubated at 37 °C for 50 min, resulting in the formation of GSH@CuNCs.

The research conducted in this study followed the regulations set by the Egyptian government and received approval from the Institutional Human Ethics Committee at Assiut University in Egypt. Plasma samples (3 mL) were combined with varying concentrations of P2O7 4− and then treated with 1.5 mL of trichloroacetic acid. The mixture was subsequently centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 20 min, and the upper portion (serum) was carefully transferred to a clean test tube and stored in a refrigerator until further use. Urine samples from our study groups were also mixed with different concentrations of P2O7 4− and then subjected to centrifugation at 4000 rpm for 10 min before filtration. River water samples were collected from various locations in the Ibrahimiya canal, Assiut city. These samples underwent filtration to eliminate insoluble and suspended particles. Following filtration, the water samples were spiked with varying concentrations of P2O7 4− and stored in a refrigerator.

The morphology of the as-prepared Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs was analyzed using TEM. Fig. 1 A illustrates the TEM image of Al-N@CQDs, displaying an average diameter of 2.8 ± 0.04 nm (n = 60) as depicted in Fig. 1B. The TEM image of GSH@CuNCs was demonstrated in Fig. 1C, which exhibits an average diameter of 3.3 nm ± 0.06 nm (n = 60) as shown in Fig. 1D. To investigate the modification of N@CQDs and CuNCs with (Al) and (GSH), respectively, FTIR technique was employed, as shown in Fig. 2 . In Fig. 2A, the FTIR spectrum of N@CQDs reveals the presence of absorption bands at 3480 cm−1, 3290 cm−1, 1710 cm−1, and 1590 cm−1, which correspond to υ (OH), υ (NH), υ (CO), and δ (OH, NH), respectively [33,34]. These bands disappear/decrease upon coordination interaction with (Al), indicating the doping of N@CQDs with (Al). Furthermore, a new absorption band appears at 580 cm−1, which can be attributed to the υ (N-Al-O), confirming the successful interaction between N@CQDs and Al. Fig. 2 B displays the FTIR spectra of CuNCs and GSH@CuNCs. It was found that the absorption band corresponding to υ (SH) at 2380 cm−1 appeared in the spectrum of GSH@CuNCs, suggesting the modification of CuNCs with GSH. Fig. 3
A displays the XRD pattern of Al-N@CQDs, revealing a diffraction peak at 23.6°, corresponding to the (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon [35,36]. In Fig. 3 B, the XRD pattern of GSH@CuNCs exhibits diffraction peaks at 23.9°, 37.8°, 51.8°, and 68.7°, which can be attributed to the (002), (111), (200), and (220) crystallographic planes, respectively [37]. To study the surface functional groups of Al-N@GQDs and GSH@CuNCs, XPS was employed. Fig. S1 A presents the XPS survey spectrum of Al-N@GQDs, showing distinctive peaks at 533.8 eV, 392.8 eV, 296.7 eV, and 74.7 eV, which correspond to O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and Al 2p, respectively. Fig. S1 B exhibits the high-resolution XPS (HR-XPS) spectrum of Al 2p, displaying sharp peaks at 75.4 eV and 77.4 eV, corresponding to Al+x and Al+3, respectively [38]. Fig. S2 A presents the XPS survey spectrum of GSH@CuNCs, showing corresponding peaks at 952.7 eV, 538.3 eV, 388.5 eV, 293.8 eV, and 192.3 eV, which correspond to Cu 2p, O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, and S 2p, respectively. Fig. S2 B displays the HR-XPS spectrum of S 2p, indicating sharp peaks at 162.4 eV and 163.7 eV, corresponding to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively. Fig. S2 C reveals the HR-XPS spectrum of Cu 2p, displaying distinctive peaks at 931.2 eV and 954.7 eV, which correspond to Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2, respectively [39].

The stability of the synthesized Al-N@GQDs and GSH@CuNCs was investigated under various conditions, including temperature, ionic strength, UV-irradiation time, and pH value. The results are presented in Fig. S3 for Al-N@GQDs and Fig. S4 for GSH@CuNCs. For Al-N@GQDs, it was observed that they could withstand variations in ionic strength, UV irradiation time, and pH value within the ranges of (0.1–2.5 M), (5–200 min), and (4.0–10.0), respectively. However, the stability of Al-N@GQDs was affected by temperature; therefore, room temperature was preferred to carry out experiments involving CQDs [14]. Similarly, the stability of GSH@CuNCs was investigated under the same conditions as Al-N@GQDs. The results indicated that GSH@CuNCs exhibited stability within the ranges of (0.1–3.0 M) for ionic strength, (5–300 min) for UV irradiation time, (4.0–8.0) for pH, and (25–40)°C for temperature. The higher stability of GSH@CuNCs can be attributed to the presence of GSH on the surface of the nanoclusters, which provides additional protection against various environmental conditions [26–28].

The absorption and fluorescence spectra of N@CQDs, Al-N@CQDs, and GSH@CuNCs were investigated as shown in Fig. 4 . In Fig. 4 A, the absorption bands of N@CQDs were observed at three different wavelengths: 218 nm, 276 nm, and 320 nm. These bands can be attributed to the following transitions: the π-π* transition of CC, trapping of excited state energy of the surface states, and the n-π* transition, respectively [40,41]. Upon doping CQDs with Al3+ ions, the absorption band at 218 nm vanished, indicating a change in the electronic structure. Additionally, the absorption band at 320 nm exhibited a blue shift, suggesting a modification in the energy levels of the material. The UV/Vis absorption spectrum of GSH@CuNCs revealed a broad absorption band ranging from 255 nm to 290 nm, which can be attributed to the Cu-S bond. This observation is consistent with a previous report [42]. Moving on to Fig. 4B, the fluorescence spectra of N@CQDs, Al-N@CQDs, and GSH@CuNCs were examined. It was evident that the as-prepared N@CQDs exhibited a fluorescence emission band at 450 nm. Interestingly, upon interaction with Al3+ ions, the fluorescence emission band underwent an 8 nm blue shift. On the other hand, GSH@CuNCs did not display any fluorescence emission band within the range of 350 nm to 750 nm. Fig. S5 illustrates the fluorescence emission characteristics of Al-N@CQDs under various excitation wavelengths ranging from 260 nm to 350 nm. It was observed that the fluorescence emission intensity increased as the excitation wavelength increased until reaching an optimum value at 310 nm. Furthermore, it was determined that the emission wavelength remained constant regardless of the excitation wavelength. Based on these findings, an excitation wavelength of 310 nm was chosen as the optimum value for Al-N@CQDs excitation. To determine the fluorescence quantum yields of N@CQDs, Al-N@CQDs, and GSH@CuNCs, the values were calculated relative to quinine sulfate (with a quantum yield of 54%), using an equation described in detail in the supporting information. The calculated fluorescence quantum yields were found to be 34.8% for N@CQDs, 50.3% for Al-N@CQDs, and 24.5% for GSH@CuNCs.

To achieve the maximum fluorescence emission from Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs, it is crucial to optimize the fabrication conditions, including the synthesis temperature, synthesis time, and the ratio of precursors. The optimization of precursor ratios was performed during the synthesis of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs, and the results are presented in Fig. S6. From Fig. S6A, it was determined that the optimum ratio between ciprofloxacin and Al3+, which resulted in the maximum fluorescence emission, is 1.75. This indicates that a specific stoichiometric balance between ciprofloxacin and Al3+ ions is necessary to achieve the highest fluorescence intensity in Al-N@CQDs. Regarding GSH@CuNCs, as they do not exhibit fluorescence on their own; the molar ratio of GSH to Cu2+ was optimized in the presence of Al3+. Fig. S6B demonstrates that the optimum fluorescence emission was obtained when the molar ratio between GSH and Cu2+ was 4:1. This finding suggests that a specific ratio between GSH and Cu2+, in the presence of Al3+, is crucial for maximizing the fluorescence emission of GSH@CuNCs. These optimization results highlight the importance of precise control over the precursor ratios during the fabrication process to achieve the maximum fluorescence emission from Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs.

The investigation of key factors influencing the reaction between P2O7 4− and the hybrid of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs was carried out, as depicted in Fig. S7. These factors include pH value, the molar ratio of Al-N@CQDs to GSH@CuNCs, and reaction time. The results led to the determination of the optimal conditions for this reaction. In Fig. S7A, the impact of pH value on the reaction was examined, and it was concluded that the optimum pH for the reaction is 6.0. At acidic pH levels, the protonation of amino groups hindered the coordination interaction between Al3+ ions and amino/carboxyl groups. On the other hand, at higher pH values, Al3+ ions could precipitate as hydroxides, leading to an unfavorable reaction environment. Fig. S7B illustrates the influence of the molar ratio of Al-N@CQDs to GSH@CuNCs on the reaction. It was determined that the best molar ratio was achieved at 1.5:1, indicating the importance of maintaining a specific balance between the two components for optimal reaction outcomes. The effect of reaction time between Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs hybrid and P2O7 4− was explored in Fig. S7C. It was found that the optimum reaction occurred after 2 min of incubation. This suggests that a relatively short reaction time is sufficient for the desired reaction to take place effectively. Based on these findings, the recommended optimal reaction conditions for measuring P2O7 4− are as follows: pH 6.0, molar ratio of Al-N@CQDs to GSH@CuNCs of 1.5:1, and a reaction time of 2 min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Aggregation-induced emission (AIE) and fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs
ce:section.ce:para: Recent findings have revealed that aluminum ions (Al3+) have the ability to trigger AIE in GSH@CuNCs. This can be attributed to the strong coordination interaction between Al3+ ions and the amino and carboxyl groups present on the surface of the protective agent GSH [43,44]. Additionally, the doping of CQDs with metal ions is facilitated by the electron-rich groups on the surface of CQDs (acting as Lewis bases) and the vacant orbitals of metal ions, such as Al3+ (acting as Lewis acids) [45,46]. To validate this concept, we conducted the following experiments. Fig. S8A presents the fluorescence spectra of GSH@CuNCs, Al-GSH@CuNCs, N@CQDs, Al-N@CQDs, and Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs. The results demonstrate that the initially non-fluorescent GSH@CuNCs exhibit significant fluorescence upon the introduction of Al3+ ions, owing to the coordination interaction between Al3+ ions (Lewis acid) and the amino/carboxyl groups (Lewis base) on the surface of GSH. Notably, the emission band of N@CQDs at 450 nm remains unchanged upon the addition of GSH@CuNCs. However, the emission of N@CQDs becomes favorable only after the addition of Al3+ ions, indicating complexation with the amino/hydroxyl groups on the surface of N@CQDs. Surprisingly, the mixture of Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs exhibits two emission peaks at 450 nm (Al-N@CQDs) and 620 nm (GSH@CuNCs), indicating the AIE effect of Al-N@CQDs inducing the aggregation of GSH@CuNCs. Fig. S8B illustrates the fluorescence emission of the Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system under different excitation wavelengths. Interestingly, it was observed that the emission fluorescence wavelengths remained constant regardless of the variation in excitation wavelengths. Fig. S8C illustrates the UV/Vis behavior of the Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system. The results clearly indicate that an increase in the volume of Al-N@CQDs corresponds to higher absorbance values. This observation can be attributed to the background scattering, providing further evidence of the aggregation of GSH@CuNCs induced by Al-N@CQDs. Moreover, the introduction of Al-N@CQDs to GSH@CuNCs led to a notable increase in the particle diameter, reaching approximately 68.9 nm (Fig. S8D). This increase in size can be attributed to the aggregation facilitated by the presence of Al-N@CQDs. Furthermore, Zeta potential measurements were employed to investigate the interaction between Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs (Fig. S9A). The Zeta potential of N@CQDs was determined to be −12.1 mV, which can be attributed to the presence of electron-rich groups, such as amino and carboxyl groups. Upon the addition of Al3+ ions, the surface charge of N@CQDs became positively charged, measuring +3.8 mV. Conversely, the Zeta potential of GSH@CuNCs was found to be negative at −11.3 mV, mainly due to the electron-rich groups present in GSH, including amino and carboxyl groups. When Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs were mixed, a hybrid structure was formed with a negative charge of −2.4 mV, resulting from the electrostatic interaction between the positively charged Al-N@CQDs and the negatively charged GSH@CuNCs. This closer interaction facilitated the coordination interaction between the Al3+ ions and GSH on the surface of CuNCs. Fig. S9B illustrates the normalized UV/Vis spectra of GSH@CuNCs and the normalized fluorescence spectra of Al-N@CQDs. Notably, a significant overlap is observed between the excitation/emission of Al-N@CQDs and the absorption of GSH@CuNCs, providing evidence for the potential involvement of the inner-filter effect or FRET mechanism. To further investigate this phenomenon, the fluorescence lifetimes of Al-N@CQDs were measured both before and after mixing with GSH@CuNCs (Fig. S9C). The results revealed a fluorescence lifetime of 8.78 ns for Al-N@CQDs alone, whereas after the mixing process, the fluorescence lifetime increased to 16.85 ns. These findings strongly suggest the occurrence of a FRET process [47].

The Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs hybrid was prepared and utilized to measure the concentration of P2O7 4−. In Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence emission spectra of the hybrid were observed after successive addition of P2O7 4− in the range of 0.035–160 µM. It was observed that the emission signal at 620 nm experienced a significant decrease, while the band at 450 nm exhibited an increase along with a red-shift in the emission wavelength. This change can be attributed to the disruption of both AIE and FRET mechanisms during the coordination interaction between Al3+ ions and P2O7 4−. Additionally, the binding of Al3+ ions with amino/carboxyl groups present on the surface of N@CQDs was weakened, resulting in slight red-shifting of the fluorescence emission wavelength. Fig. 5B illustrates the calibration plot depicting the response ratio (F450/F620) as a function of the concentration of P2O7 4−. A linear relationship was observed (n = 6), and the regression equation derived from the data is as follows: F450/F620 = 1.15 (±0.01) + 0.028 (±2 × 10–4) CP2O74- (R2 = 0.9987). To determine the detection limit (LOD) with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3:1, the calculation was based on a standard deviation of the blank readings of 0.0001. Consequently, the LOD was determined to be 0.012 µM. The analytical performance of the designed system was compared to other sensors reported for P2O7 4− determination, as summarized in Table 1 . The system demonstrated several notable advantages over the other sensors, including a wide linear range, a low detection limit, a short response time, and improved reliability due to its ratiometric response.

The reproducibility of the Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system was assessed by conducting five separate batches using identical conditions. The relative standard deviation (RSD %) was determined to be 3.78%, indicating a high degree of reproducibility for the prepared system. Additionally, the stability of the system was investigated by storing the Al-N@CQDs and GSH@CuNCs at 4 °C for a period of 33 days, with measurements taken every 8 days. Remarkably, even after this extended storage period, the system maintained a significant level of activity, with a retention rate of 95.87%.

To determine the suitability of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system for practical detection of P2O7 4−, selectivity and interference studies were conducted (Fig. 6 ). In the presence or absence of P2O7 4− (10 μM), various potential cations (at a concentration of 50 mM Ba2+, 1 mM for Na+, K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+), anions (at a concentration of 20 μM), and biomolecules (at a concentration of 100 µM) were individually introduced to the probe. Comparing the results with the blank, the fluorescence ratio (F450/F620) showed minimal changes. This indicates that the addition of individual cations and anions did not significantly affect the fluorescence response, confirming the probe's selectivity for Pi detection and minimal interference from other species.

The Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs system was employed to determine the P2O7 4− anion in various matrices, including water, serum, and urine samples, in order to demonstrate its potential applications. The results obtained from these samples using the as-fabricated systems are summarized in Table 2 . The obtained results exhibited satisfactory performance, ranging from 95.9% to 104.0%, thereby confirming the practical viability of the proposed system.

In this study, the coordination capacity of Al3+ and the intrinsic fluorescence property of N@CQDs were effectively preserved in the fabrication of Al-N@CQDs. This unique combination allowed Al-N@CQDs to serve as an inducer for activating non-emissive GSH@CuNCs, leading to an AIE effect through a FRET process. Consequently, a hybrid of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs with dual emission was formed and utilized as a ratiometric probe for the detection of P2O7 4− anion. The detection mechanism relied on the competitive interaction between Al3+ and P2O7 4− anion. Upon the addition of P2O7 4−, both the AIE and FRET processes were disrupted, resulting in the restoration of fluorescence emission at 450 nm and quenching at 620 nm, respectively. The hybrid probe exhibited a good linear relationship with P2O7 4− concentration in the range of 0.035–160 µM, with a limit of detection of 0.012 μM. This strategy distinguishes itself from other approaches used in designing nanostructures. A hybrid of Al-N@CQDs/GSH@CuNCs was easily formed through a simple mixture of the two materials, harnessing the AIE effect and FRET process. This approach significantly reduced the need for complex synthetic procedures, streamlining the fabrication process. Moreover, the total analysis time for this method was remarkably short, requiring only 4 min, which greatly enhanced the detection efficiency. Additionally, the strategy of forming hybrids with CQDs expanded the application potential of thiolate-capped nanoclusters due to the wide range of possibilities offered by different types of CQDs.

Ashraf M. Mahmoud: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Methodology, Software, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Samer S. Abu-Alrub: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Resources, Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft. Ali O. Al-Qarni: Data curation, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Software, Validation, Visualization. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: . Reem Y. Shahin: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Software, Visualization, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Deanship of Scientific Research at Najran University for funding this work under the National Research Priorities funding program grant code (NU/NRP/MRC/12/19).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.123850.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective detection of pyrophosphate anions in aqueous medium using aggregation of perylene diimide as a fluorescent probe

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Dey, P.K. Sukul, Selective detection of pyrophosphate anions in aqueous medium using aggregation of perylene diimide as a fluorescent probe. ACS Omega, 4 (2019), pp. 16191-16200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A turn-on luminescence probe based on amino-functionalized metal-organic frameworks for the selective detections of Cu2+, Pb2+ and pyrophosphate
Spectrochim. Acta Part A: Mol. Biomol. Spectros.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. An, Q. Tan, S. Pan, H. Liu, X. Hu, A turn-on luminescence probe based on amino-functionalized metal-organic frameworks for the selective detections of Cu2+, Pb2+ and pyrophosphate. Spectrochim. Acta Part A: Mol. Biomol. Spectros., 247 (2021), Article 119073.
